questions.scenario:
During a deploy, an operator stops a report exporter with the default signal:

```bash
$ kill 18472
$ sleep 20
$ ps -o pid,stat,cmd -p 18472
  PID STAT CMD
18472 S    /usr/local/bin/report-exporter
$ kill -9 18472
$ ps -p 18472
  PID TTY          TIME CMD
```

The operator says the first command proved that `kill` is unreliable on this host. What is the most accurate explanation?

options.text: The default `kill` signal is `SIGTERM`, which the process can catch, delay, or ignore while it cleans up. `SIGKILL` cannot be caught or ignored, so the kernel terminated it without giving the process a cleanup path.
The default `kill` command only signals the current shell session. `kill -9` is the first command that actually targets the process ID.
The process was already a zombie. `SIGKILL` reaped the zombie after the parent failed to call `wait()`.
The default signal requires root privileges, while `SIGKILL` can be sent by any user that knows the PID.

questions.explanation: Plain `kill` sends `SIGTERM` by default. That is a polite shutdown request, not a guaranteed removal, and a service can install a handler to drain work or even ignore it. `SIGKILL` is different because the kernel does not deliver it to user-space handlers. A zombie is already dead and cannot be killed, and signal permissions do not become looser just because the signal number is 9.

questions.scenario:
A Kubernetes worker writes checkpoints during shutdown. The application logs show the handler starts, but checkpoints are often missing after rolling updates:

```text
2025-04-19T10:15:01Z signal=SIGTERM msg="begin checkpoint flush" estimated_seconds=45
2025-04-19T10:15:31Z kubelet msg="Killing container with a grace period expired"
```

The pod spec sets `terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 30`. What change best fits the evidence?

options.text: Increase the termination grace period so the `SIGTERM` handler has time to flush before Kubernetes escalates to `SIGKILL`.
Trap `SIGKILL` and run the same checkpoint code from that handler.
Replace the handler with `SIGSTOP`, because stopped processes keep their memory and can flush later.
Remove the `SIGTERM` handler so the kernel closes all files in a deterministic order.

questions.explanation: The article describes Kubernetes sending `SIGTERM` first, waiting for the grace period, then using `SIGKILL`. The log shows the grace period is shorter than the expected flush, so the kernel eventually kills the process before cleanup finishes. `SIGKILL` cannot be trapped, `SIGSTOP` only pauses the process, and removing the handler would make graceful shutdown less reliable.

questions.scenario:
A production web worker refuses to disappear during an incident:

```bash
$ ps -o pid,stat,wchan,cmd -p 8112
  PID STAT WCHAN  CMD
 8112 D    nfs_wait nginx: worker process
$ kill -9 8112
$ ps -o pid,stat,wchan,cmd -p 8112
  PID STAT WCHAN  CMD
 8112 D    nfs_wait nginx: worker process
```

What should the incident lead investigate first?

options.text: The process is in uninterruptible sleep waiting on kernel I/O. Fix the blocked I/O path, such as NFS or disk, so the pending signal can take effect.
nginx workers catch `SIGKILL` by default. Send the signal to the master process instead.
The PID belongs to a thread, so Linux queues `SIGKILL` until every thread receives a matching signal.
The operator used the wrong signal number. `SIGKILL` is 15 on Linux and `SIGTERM` is 9.

questions.explanation: The `D` state is uninterruptible sleep. A process stuck there is inside a kernel wait, commonly storage or network filesystem I/O, and signals are acted on only when the process reaches a safe point again. nginx cannot catch `SIGKILL`, thread targeting is not the issue shown here, and the signal numbers are reversed in the wrong option.

questions.scenario:
A container uses this entrypoint:

```dockerfile
ENTRYPOINT ["/bin/sh", "-c", "python /srv/worker.py"]
```

The Python code has a `SIGTERM` handler that drains work. During node maintenance, the handler never logs anything, and the pod exits only after the runtime sends a final kill signal. Which root cause best matches the article?

options.text: The shell is PID 1 in the container. PID 1 has special signal behavior, and most shells do not forward shutdown signals to children, so the Python process never receives the graceful request.
Python processes cannot receive signals inside Linux PID namespaces unless they run as root.
Kubernetes sends shutdown signals only to the host PID, not to the container namespace.
`SIGTERM` is generated only by interactive terminals, so a runtime cannot use it during pod shutdown.

questions.explanation: The article calls out the container PID 1 trap. A shell wrapper can become PID 1, ignore default termination behavior, and fail to forward signals to the actual workload. Running the app directly, using `exec` in the wrapper, or adding a tiny init like `tini` fixes that path. The other options contradict Linux signal and namespace behavior described in the article.

questions.scenario:
A host records this kernel message and the service supervisor immediately starts a replacement process:

```text
kernel: Out of memory: Killed process 26631 (java) total-vm:8145152kB, anon-rss:5120936kB, file-rss:0kB, shmem-rss:0kB
```

The application team asks why its shutdown hook did not close a batch cleanly. What is the best answer?

options.text: The OOM killer terminates the selected process as if a force kill occurred, so user-space shutdown hooks do not get a chance to run.
The OOM killer sends `SIGTERM`, but Java ignores it unless the process has a foreground terminal.
The kernel converts all OOM exits into zombies until PID 1 runs the application's shutdown hook.
The supervisor caused the missing cleanup by restarting too quickly before the original process handled `SIGINT`.

questions.explanation: The process-management article says the OOM killer makes the process disappear like someone ran `kill -9`, with the record in `dmesg` or the journal. That means cleanup hooks and signal handlers are bypassed. This is not a terminal issue, not zombie reaping, and not a `SIGINT` restart race.

questions.scenario:
A background helper exits, but it remains visible in the process table:

```bash
$ ps -o pid,ppid,stat,cmd -p 30241
  PID  PPID STAT CMD
30241 29810 Z    [sync-helper] <defunct>
$ kill -9 30241
$ ps -o pid,ppid,stat,cmd -p 30241
  PID  PPID STAT CMD
30241 29810 Z    [sync-helper] <defunct>
```

What fix should the owner prioritize?

options.text: Fix or restart the parent process so it calls `wait()` and reaps the child's exit status.
Send `SIGSTOP` before `SIGKILL`, because zombies must be paused before they can be removed.
Raise the child's nice priority so it can finish the remaining cleanup work.
Delete the matching `/proc` directory so the kernel frees the PID slot.

questions.explanation: A zombie has already exited. It is waiting only for its parent to collect the exit code with `wait()`, so no signal can kill it again. The parent must reap it, or be restarted so PID 1 adopts and reaps the zombie. Nice values and manual `/proc` deletion do not solve a reaping bug.

questions.scenario: A daemon starts fire-and-forget image thumbnail helpers. The parent sets `SIGCHLD` to `SIG_IGN` to avoid zombie buildup. Later, a debug build tries to call `wait()` after forking a helper and consistently gets `ECHILD` even though the helper just exited. What is the most accurate explanation?

options.text: Ignoring `SIGCHLD` tells the kernel to auto-reap children, so there is no child exit status left for `wait()` to collect.
`wait()` only works for foreground children. Background helpers must be collected through job control.
`ECHILD` means the child is still running and the parent needs to retry without blocking.
The parent must send itself `SIGHUP` before it can see child exit codes.

questions.explanation: The article explains the `SIGCHLD` escape hatch: `SIG_IGN` or `SA_NOCLDWAIT` can ask the kernel to reap children automatically. That prevents zombies, but it also discards the exit status that `wait()` would normally collect. The issue is not job control, a retry mode, or `SIGHUP`.

questions.scenario: You are reviewing a shutdown design for a containerized batch processor. It must flush a small queue, should be safe during normal rollouts, and has no need to preserve work after an OOM kill. Which review comments are correct? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: Handle `SIGTERM` and make the handler finish the queue flush quickly enough for the configured grace period.
Handle `SIGKILL` as the final fallback so the same cleanup runs even after the grace period expires.
Avoid a shell wrapper as PID 1 unless it uses `exec` or a tiny init that forwards signals and reaps children.
Treat `SIGINT` consistently with `SIGTERM` if operators run the same process interactively during tests.
Assume an OOM kill will run the normal signal handler, then rely on the supervisor restart for cleanup.

questions.explanation: A graceful container shutdown depends on catching `SIGTERM`, having enough grace time, and making sure the real workload receives the signal. Treating `SIGINT` similarly is good operational hygiene for interactive runs. `SIGKILL` and OOM kills bypass user-space handlers, so they cannot be used for a cleanup path.

questions.scenario: An incident review lists several proposed signal-handling facts for the runbook. Which statements are accurate enough to keep? (Select all that apply.)

options.text: `SIGKILL` and `SIGSTOP` are the two signals user code cannot catch, block, or ignore.
`SIGTERM` is the default signal sent by `kill`, and it is intended as a polite shutdown request.
Long-lived processes in `D` state may remain visible after `SIGKILL` until the blocked kernel I/O returns.
A zombie should be removed by sending `SIGKILL` to the zombie PID before checking the parent.
If a process catches or ignores `SIGTERM`, immediate escalation to `SIGKILL` is always safer than allowing a short drain period.

questions.explanation: The correct runbook notes match the article: `SIGKILL` and `SIGSTOP` are non-negotiable, default `kill` sends `SIGTERM`, and `D` state can make even a force kill appear stuck until I/O unblocks. A zombie is already dead and needs its parent to reap it. Escalating immediately to `SIGKILL` can lose in-flight work, so the article recommends trying graceful termination first and using force kill last.
